title: Grammar basics
description: A short Rule 4 lesson: preferred order, particles, serial want+go, bare destinations, and casual Actor drop. Uses the live lexicon.

exercises.tip: Present has no time particle. Past = ta · Future = sa. The action word never changes.
Put ta next to the action for past. Still: Actor → Action → Target.
Particles are tiny and closed: mi, ta, sa, no, ya, von. Type only the grammar particles you see.
exercises.promptLang: Type the grammar particles in: mi sa no sak lo

exercises.tip: Negation no sits in front of the action: mi no sak …
Want + go stacks in the Action: sak gi. Do not park the second verb as the object.
exercises.promptLang: Put these in order: gi · sak · mi · ye
exercises.answerLang: I want to go to the water.

exercises.tip: Plain “go to X” needs no nan. Destination sits bare after the motion.
exercises.promptLang: Which means “I go to the water”?

exercises.tip: Use nan only for real direction contrast — toward / from / away — not for plain “to”.
exercises.promptLang: I want to go toward the water.
exercises.answerLang: I want to go toward the water.

exercises.tip: Preferred order: Actor → Action → Place. Serial want+go, bare beach.
exercises.promptLang: Do you want to go to the beach?
exercises.answerLang: Do you want to go to the beach?

exercises.tip: In casual yes/no to “you”, Actor be can drop. Full form still includes it.
exercises.promptLang: Which is also OK for “want to go to the beach?” (casual)
exercises.answerLang: Want to go to the beach?

exercises.tip: Words never inflect. Tribe and war are compounds from roots — same spelling every time.
